questions.question: Which built-in function creates a managed attribute by accepting getter, setter, and deleter functions as arguments?
questions.explanation: The property() built-in creates a managed attribute. It accepts up to four arguments: fget, fset, fdel, and doc. For example: name = property(get_name, set_name, del_name, 'name doc').

questions.question: Which decorator syntax creates a read-only property when no setter is defined?
questions.explanation: Using @property on a method makes it the getter for a managed attribute. Without a corresponding @name.setter, any assignment attempt raises AttributeError, making it effectively read-only.

questions.question: A descriptor class must define at least one of the methods __get__, __set__, or ___.
questions.explanation: The descriptor protocol consists of three optional methods: __get__ for attribute access, __set__ for attribute assignment, and __delete__ for attribute deletion. A class defining any of these becomes a descriptor.

questions.question: What is the key difference between __getattr__ and __getattribute__?
choices: __getattr__ runs for every attribute access; __getattribute__ only for missing ones
__getattr__ is called only when normal lookup fails; __getattribute__ is called for every attribute access
questions.explanation: __getattr__ is a fallback called only when an attribute is not found through normal mechanisms. __getattribute__ intercepts every attribute access unconditionally, making it more powerful but requiring care to avoid infinite recursion.

questions.question: In the descriptor protocol, what arguments does the __get__ method receive?
choices: self, the instance, and the owner class
self, the attribute name, and the value
questions.explanation: The __get__(self, instance, owner) method receives the descriptor instance, the object instance the attribute was accessed on (or None if accessed via the class), and the owner class.

questions.question: When using __setattr__ to intercept attribute assignments, how do you store a value without causing infinite recursion?
choices: Use a global variable to hold the value
Assign via self.__dict__[attr] = value or call object.__setattr__(self, attr, value)
You cannot store values when using __setattr__
questions.explanation: Since __setattr__ intercepts all attribute assignments including self.x = val, you must bypass it by writing directly to the instance's __dict__ or calling object.__setattr__() to avoid infinite recursion.
